factual-accuracy.requirement: States to hold or reduce dose of metoprolol and all AV-nodal blockers immediately (unless a cardiologist or electrophysiologist explicitly recommends otherwise)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends “immediate consideration to hold or reduce AV-nodal blockers (e.g., beta-blockers like metoprolol)” and discusses holding/reducing AV-nodal blockers with an exception to defer to specialist input in certain cases, which aligns with the criterion’s “unless a cardiologist or electrophysiologist explicitly recommends otherwise” clause.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends a specific replacement non-rate-slowing antihypertensive agent not affecting AV node (amlodipine, thiazide, or ARB) as an alternative to a AV-nodal blocker
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends switching from metoprolol to non–AV-node–blocking antihypertensives, listing specific alternatives including ARBs (e.g., losartan), thiazide-type diuretics, and dihydropyridine CCBs (e.g., amlodipine). This satisfies recommending a specific replacement non-rate-slowing agent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Implements immediate driving restriction until symptoms are controlled, physician-approved, or diagnosis established
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends a complete driving restriction for private drivers until diagnosis is established and risk is mitigated, with resumption only after specialist confirmation of low risk. This implements an immediate driving restriction tied to symptom control/physician approval/diagnostic clarification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides specific 911/emergency return precautions listing at least 2 of syncope, presyncope, HR <40, chest pain
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives explicit emergency/911 return precautions including loss of consciousness/syncope or near-syncope, heart rate <40 bpm with symptoms, and new chest pain/shortness of breath, satisfying the requirement for at least two listed triggers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends maintaining a home pulse or symptom diary or BP/HR log, and to bring in these items or knowledge atthe  next medical visit
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends a home pulse and symptom diary with instructions to record pulse and symptoms and states to bring the diary to appointments and share it with the care team to support follow-up and authorization.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies MCOT or auto-triggered extended event monitor for 14-30 days
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends ambulatory patch ECG monitoring for 14 days and also recommends MCOT with real-time notification as the preferred modality for higher-risk/socially isolated patients, which fits the 14–30 day MCOT/auto-triggered extended event monitor requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States monitor placement within 24-72 hours
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends expedited evaluation “within 24 hours” but does not state a monitor should be placed within a 24–72 hour window. It discusses ambulatory patch/MCOT and urgency generally, without specifying the 24–72 hours timeframe for monitor placement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides at least one backup option if MCOT delayed or denied (short admission telemetry stay, patch monitor, or direct EP referral)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides backup options if MCOT/patch monitoring is not available, including maximizing low-tech monitoring with frequent telephone follow-up and mentioning an implantable loop recorder as an alternative modality. It does not explicitly propose a short admission telemetry stay or direct EP referral, but it does offer at least one alternative monitoring approach.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States real-time provider notification, alerts, or 24/7 monitoring capability
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends MCOT “with real-time event notification” and notes it is preferred when “real-time escalation to medical services is desired,” which describes real-time provider alerts/monitoring capability.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Orders transthoracic echocardiogram within 1-2 weeks
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends arranging a transthoracic echocardiogram “as soon as possible,” specifying an ideal timeframe of “within 1 week,” which falls within the required 1–2 week window.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides medical necessity language mentioning progressive conduction disease, recurrent symptoms, or PR prolongation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes medical-necessity language describing progressive conduction disease (PR 200 to 240 ms/“progressive PR prolongation”) and recurrent symptoms (lightheadedness, fatigue), and it explicitly uses this phrasing in the sample insurance documentation to justify monitoring.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States real-time monitoring needed for safety due to living alone, driving, or intermittent events
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends MCOT with real-time event notification and states it is preferred when the patient lives alone and is higher risk, emphasizing real-time escalation for safety. It also includes driving restrictions due to risk of sudden incapacitation, reinforcing the need for safety-focused monitoring.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions peer-to-peer review, out-of-network request, or admission if authorization delays persist
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends an insurance escalation pathway including an “immediate resubmission with ‘peer-to-peer’ request” after denial, and it also discusses admission/ED return triggers if urgent evaluation is needed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States specific pause duration requiring urgent EP (pause ≥3 seconds symptomatic or ≥5 seconds asymptomatic)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “pauses >3 seconds” as a trigger but does not specify the guideline-relevant urgent EP thresholds of pause ≥3 seconds when symptomatic or ≥5 seconds when asymptomatic. It also does not distinguish symptomatic vs asymptomatic pause duration criteria for urgent electrophysiology/pacemaker consultation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States HR <40 bpm with symptoms requires emergent evaluation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that “Heart rate persistently <40 bpm, especially if associated with symptoms” warrants “emergent specialist evaluation, admission, or ED return,” and repeats “Heart rate <40 bpm with symptoms” as an immediate ED/911 threshold.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Lists at least 2 specific block types requiring same-day or urgent EP (Mobitz II, 2:1 block, high-grade AV block, or complete heart block)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists multiple urgent block types, explicitly naming Mobitz II, third-degree (complete) AV block, and high-grade second-degree block as triggers for expedited evaluation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States syncope or true loss of consciousness requires emergent same-day evaluation (go to emergency room or call 911)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that “any loss of consciousness or near-syncope” should prompt an “immediate ED visit or call to emergency services,” which covers emergent same-day evaluation (ER/911) for syncope/true LOC.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies EP or cardiology consultation within 72 hours or states urgent timeline
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response specifies an urgent timeline for specialist evaluation, stating to “Expedite (stat, within 24 hours) advanced in-person evaluation and monitoring” for high-risk AV block findings. This satisfies the requirement for an urgent consultation timeline (within 72 hours or urgent).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Continues metoprolol or recommends dose reduction by the patient without stopping
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends holding or reducing AV-nodal blockers like metoprolol and explicitly advises against abrupt discontinuation, favoring gradual tapering unless life-threatening instability occurs. This constitutes continuing therapy with dose reduction rather than stopping outright.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Allows continued driving without restriction despite progressive conduction disease and recurrent presyncope
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends driving restriction, stating “complete restriction until diagnosis is established and risk is mitigated” and that restrictions “are not optional and must be enforced.” It does not allow continued driving without restriction despite the conduction disease and presyncope.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Dismisses progressive AV block as benign, stable, age-related, or not concerning
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response treats the progressive AV block as concerning and repeatedly recommends urgent escalation for high-grade block, symptomatic bradycardia, and pauses, including driving restriction and expedited evaluation. It does not characterize the conduction disease as benign, stable, age-related, or not concerning.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Delays monitoring to 4 or more weeks without urgent interim plan or advising to follow-up in a clinic
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly recommends urgent/expedited evaluation (e.g., “stat, within 24 hours” for high-risk findings), frequent interim check-ins every 2–3 days, and clear ED return thresholds, rather than waiting 4+ weeks without a plan. It also emphasizes rapid escalation pathways and follow-up actions while awaiting definitive cardiology assessment.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States for intermittent symptoms occurring over weeks, of all non-invasive remote monitoring options mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry (up to 30 days) offers the highest diagnostic yield with real-time arrhythmia detection and immediate clinician notification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends MCOT with real-time event notification and notes it is preferred for higher-risk patients, but it does not state that MCOT (up to 30 days) has the highest diagnostic yield among all non-invasive remote monitoring options over weeks, nor does it explicitly mention immediate clinician notification as the key comparative advantage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that the presence of left bundle branch block on electrocardiogram markedly increases the likelihood of underlying structural heart disease and of diagnosing left ventricular systolic dysfunction, which warrants urgent echocardiography in this setting of lightheadedness and fatigue
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that new LBBB in older adults strongly suggests underlying structural heart disease, including LV systolic dysfunction, and that it mandates echocardiographic assessment with expedited/urgent follow-up in this symptomatic context.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies progressive AV block, high-grade block, or degenerative conduction disease as primary concern
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly frames the key issue as progressive AV conduction disease with intermittent dropped beats and explicitly highlights high-grade AV block (e.g., “progressive AV block,” “high-grade… second-degree block,” “Mobitz II or third-degree AV block”) as the main risk driving urgent escalation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recognizes metoprolol as a contributing factor
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly identifies beta-blockers like metoprolol as AV-nodal blockers and recommends holding or reducing them in the setting of progressive PR prolongation and new symptoms, recognizing metoprolol as a contributing factor.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States that to maximize authorization success, documentation should emphasize all of the following: (1) recurrent ED visits with symptoms potentially attributable to bradycardia, (2) progressive ECG changes (PR prolongation, dropped beats), (3) inability to capture arrhythmia during brief ED monitoring, and (4) high-risk features (elderly, lives alone, drives daily).
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response’s insurance documentation section emphasizes recurrent ED visits with bradycardic symptoms, progressive ECG changes (PR 200→240 ms with dropped beats), inability to capture events during brief ED monitoring, and high-risk features like living alone and driving risk (driving restrictions discussed).

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes response into distinct sections for assessment, immediate management, monitoring strategy, and escalation thresholds
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into multiple labeled sections that cover assessment (overview/criteria), immediate management (medication modification and urgent escalation), monitoring strategy (ambulatory and home monitoring), and escalation thresholds (urgent triggers and ED return criteria). These sections are distinct and clearly delineated with headings.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses clinical terminology such as drug names, PR intervals in ms, HR thresholds in bpm, and block type classifications
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses clinical terminology including drug names (metoprolol, lisinopril, losartan, amlodipine), PR interval values in ms (e.g., 240 ms, 200 ms), heart-rate thresholds in bpm (e.g., <40 bpm), and AV block classifications (first-degree AV block, Mobitz II, third-degree/high-grade block).

presentation-quality.requirement: Places immediate safety actions (medication holds, driving restrictions, emergency precautions) in opening or clearly labeled immediate section
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes clearly labeled early sections with immediate safety actions, such as holding/reducing AV-nodal blockers and explicit “Emergency Safety-Netting and Activity (Driving) Restrictions” guidance, including ED/911 precautions and driving restriction recommendations.

citation-quality.requirement: References the "2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients with Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay" as definitive authority: https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000628
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites the 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Bradycardia guideline multiple times and includes it in the Sources section with links, explicitly using it as the guideline basis for recommendations.
